articles.full_text:
UP Monsoon delayed, likely to arrive by June 24:Temperature set to rise sharply as heatwave intensifies; Maharajganj sees heavy rain
The spell of storms and rain across Uttar Pradesh over the past two weeks has finally subsided, with temperatures now rising sharply across the state. On Sunday, clear skies and bright sunshine were recorded in all 75 districts.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has said that with the western disturbance weakening, there are no immediate signs of rainfall. In the next 24 hours, temperatures are expected to rise by 5–6°C, potentially reaching 47–48°C in parts of the state. A heatwave warning has been issued for the next three days starting June 9.
According to the IMD, relief is likely only after June 11, when pre-monsoon showers may begin again. The monsoon, however, is expected to arrive in Uttar Pradesh around June 24—4 to 6 days later than usual—and is likely to cover the entire state within 10 days of its onset.
Earlier on Saturday, several districts including Varanasi, Kaushambi, Mau, Ballia and Ghazipur witnessed heavy rain, strong winds of up to 50 kmph, and lightning activity. Gorakhpur also experienced a dust storm that brought temporary relief from the heat. In Amethi, a tragic incident occurred when a mud wall collapsed, killing a mother and daughter and seriously injuring a son.
On Saturday, Jhansi remained the hottest city in the state at 42.5°C, followed by Banda (42.2°C), Jalaun and Fatehpur (41.4°C each), and Varanasi (41.2°C). Azamgarh recorded the lowest night temperature at 20°C.
Senior scientist Atul Kumar Singh said-
All systems of storms and rain in UP have ended. Currently, the weather is likely to remain dry. There will be intense heat until pre-monsoon rains. Heat wave conditions will prevail. Monsoon may enter the state via Gorakhpur route by June 24. Indications are that the monsoon may also weaken in 20 to 25 days.
Temperature in Kanpur on Saturday was 38.2 degrees Celsius. Hot winds troubled people.
Temperature in Kanpur on Saturday was 38.2 degrees Celsius. Hot winds troubled people.
What will the weather be like for the next 3 days? Know here
June 7: There is a possibility of light rain across the state. Bright sunshine will appear intermittently, which may increase humidity.
June 8: Weather will remain clear across the entire state. Bright sunshine will appear. There is no possibility of rain.
June 9: Bright sunshine will appear in most districts across the state. Winds may blow at a speed of 30 km per hour. This will provide relief from humidity.
Wind will blow at a speed of 60-70 KM along with rain in these districts
Saharanpur, Shamli, Muzaffarnagar, Baghpat, Meerut, Ghaziabad, Hapur, Gautam Budh Nagar, Bulandshahr, Aligarh, Mathura, Hathras, Bijnor, Amroha, Moradabad, Sambhal
Nautapa did not scorch in UP this time, temperature dropped by 10C
Talking about Nautapa, it remained ineffective in UP this time. Due to storms, rain and hailstorms in many districts of the state, the temperature dropped by about 10C. On the first day of Nautapa, i.e., May 25, the mercury had reached 47.6C, which fell to 38C. However, now the effect of western disturbance has started ending. This will increase the heat again. The mercury will cross 45C.
There will be less rainfall during monsoon in UP, know the reason
Between June and September, Uttar Pradesh is also expected to receive below-normal rainfall. Dr. M Mahapatra, Director General of the Meteorological Department, said that this year there are signs of La Nina-like conditions in the Pacific Ocean ending and moving towards El Nino.
Which will cause less rainfall. This means there are indications that the south-west monsoon will be weaker than normal this year. Additionally, the reduced snow formation in the Northern Hemisphere during January-March this year may also affect the monsoon.
Last year monsoon was 10 to 15% more than normal
According to the Meteorological Department, during the 2025 monsoon season (June to September) in Uttar Pradesh, an average rainfall of 870 to 900 mm was recorded. This was approximately 10 to 15 percent more than the normal monsoon average.
